figures.gloss: The supreme maker of the BaKongo — Nzambi a Mpungu, 'the Most High Nzambi,' who fashioned the world and the first people and then withdrew above the firmament, neither prayed to nor offered to in daily life, for he is too vast to be approached directly.
figures.facet: The creator who leaves NO cult behind: the BaKongo address the bakisi and the ancestors, never Nzambi himself, holding that what is everywhere needs no shrine. Divinity proven by its very absence from the altar.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed from Kongo oral tradition and the writings of Wyatt MacGaffey, Karl Laman and John Janzen; 17th-c. missionary records already note the high-god's remoteness.

figures.gloss: The cross-in-a-circle — a vertical line through a horizontal one, ringed by the sun's four moments (musoni dawn, kala noon, luvemba dusk, tukula midnight). Above is the land of the living, below the land of the dead, and the line between is Kalunga, the water that mirrors.
figures.facet: Not a deity but a DIAGRAM of the soul's whole circuit: the four points are the four stages of a single life and a single sun — birth, maturity, death, rebirth — so the cross encodes reincarnation as a clockwise turning of the same wheel. The cosmos read as one revolving glyph.
figures.provenance: The dikenga / yowa cross documented by MacGaffey and Robert Farris Thompson ('Flash of the Spirit'); attested in nkisi practice, gravesite art and diasporic Kongo survivals (Cuba, Haiti, the American South).

figures.gloss: The great water — the horizontal line of the cosmogram, the sea and the threshold both. Kalunga is the mirror-surface dividing the living world above from the inverted world of the dead below, where the BaKongo say day and night, our seasons and theirs, run in reverse.
figures.facet: Death imagined not as a downward fall but as a CROSSING and an INVERSION: to die is to pass through the water-line and walk a world that is the photographic negative of this one. The grave and the ocean are the same door — a reading that armed the enslaved with the belief that the sea-passage could be re-crossed home.
figures.provenance: Kalunga as both 'sea/abyss' and 'death-line' is widely attested across Bantu languages (Kongo, Mbundu, Ndonga); the mirror-inversion of the dead's world is core to MacGaffey's account of Kongo cosmology.

figures.gloss: The simbi (plural bisimbi) — local spirits of springs, rivers, pools and stones, the nearer powers that inhabit the watery places along Kalunga's edge and govern the fortunes of a clan's territory.
figures.facet: Where the creator recedes, the WATER fills with presences: the BaKongo people their landscape with the bisimbi so that the sacred is never far — a divinity dispersed into every spring rather than enthroned in one heaven.
figures.provenance: Bisimbi documented by Laman and MacGaffey; carried into the diaspora as 'Simbi' loa in Haitian Vodou and as water-spirits in Gullah and Lowcountry tradition.

figures.gloss: The nkisi (plural minkisi) — a sacred container bound with medicines and earth from the grave, animated by a captured spirit of the dead and directed by the nganga ritual-expert to heal, to judge, or to strike. The nail-studded nkondi is its most famous form.
figures.facet: A portable axis between worlds: the nkisi works precisely because it holds graveyard matter — it is a node where the land of the dead is folded into a single object, the cosmogram made graspable in the hand. Ritual not as appeal to a far god but as engineering of the near dead.
figures.provenance: Minkisi extensively recorded in Laman's Kongo texts and MacGaffey's translations of nganga testimony; many nkondi figures survive in European museums from the colonial seizures of the 1880s-1900s.

figures.gloss: The clan dead who dwell across Kalunga in the village below the water, white-skinned in the inverted land, still members of the lineage — consulted, fed, and depended upon to send back their own as newborn children.
figures.facet: The dead are the engine of the living: in the cosmogram's turning, the bakulu below are not an end-state but the supply of souls returning above. Ancestry is a tide, not a terminus.
figures.provenance: Bakulu / bambuta in Kongo ethnography (MacGaffey, Janzen); the whiteness of the dead underlies the early Kongo identification of returning Europeans-from-the-sea with ancestors.

figures.gloss: In a Kongo-Angolan myth, the first human Mahungu was whole and complete, both male and female together, until a wind at the palm-tree of the world split the being into Lumbu the woman and Luwawa the man, who forever seek to rejoin.
figures.facet: The androgyne BEFORE difference: completeness is the original state and division the fall, so union (marriage, ritual, the rejoined cosmogram) is a homecoming to a wholeness once held. A complementarity remembered as loss, not as eternal balance.
figures.provenance: The Mahungu split-androgyne myth recorded among Kongo and neighbouring Angolan peoples; less universally attested than the cosmogram and flagged here as regional rather than pan-Kongo.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The highly withdrawn, unapproachable supreme creator deity of Afro-diasporic cosmologies.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Activated ancestral forces that materialise to govern the community and cleanse malevolence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Sacred geometric sign-systems that literally map the cosmos and govern the circulation of spiritual power.
GOLD SEAM: Strikingly parallel concepts of an original, perfected androgyne that contains both male and female principles in absolute equilibrium.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Territorial nature spirits inhabiting specific bodies of water and earth, fiercely protective of their localized domains.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A primordial, perfectly balanced male-female pair that initiates the lineage of human history.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme high god of immense power who established the world but has since withdrawn to the sky.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Man-made power objects intentionally packed with organic, sacrificial, and symbolic matter to house and direct raw spiritual force.
GOLD SEAM: Anonymous, powerful collectives of ancestral and landscape spirits that govern tribal sovereignty without needing individual anthropomorphic myths.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Power not as abstract prayer, but as a spirit bound into a constructed material effigy that must be physically fed and maintained.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve the binding of an invisible force or spirit fragment into a physical vessel to execute the operator's specific, pragmatic commands.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme high god of immense power who established the world but has since withdrawn to the sky.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The stabilized, wise dead who act as a protective barrier around the community, distinct from chaotic natural spirits.
GOLD SEAM: The collective venerated dead who anchor the lineage, functioning as the primary mediators of blessing and social cohesion for the living.
GOLD SEAM: Reverence for the ancestral lineage as the primary source of ethical authority and the bridge between the spiritual and mundane worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial, hermaphroditic completeness that precedes separated human genders, representing a state of absolute but unsustainable cosmic wholeness.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an absolute creator who, having established the world and its natural laws, withdrew entirely from human affairs.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme, perfectly removed deities who do not intervene in the daily struggles or mechanics of the earthly realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Kongo ancestral concepts deeply inform how crossed Spiritism interacts with the dead, prioritizing physical action over mere communication.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The water in the bóveda's glasses literally represents the Kalunga line—the watery barrier between the living and the dead in Kongo-Cuban cosmology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Like the nature spirits of the Kongo, the Comisiones (such as the Indios) act as localized, environmental spiritual guides rooting practitioners to the land.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Localized nature spirits deeply tied to territorial boundaries and water sources.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are complex geometric cosmological maps tracing the dynamic transformation of energy, life, and consciousness through strictly defined developmental points.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Manufactured objects ritually charged with spirit power to provide absolute physical protection and repel enemy weaponry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both use a physical, constructed anchor to trap, direct, and utilize an autonomous spirit.
GOLD SEAM: A cross-shaped cosmic diagram dynamically traced to orient the human soul amidst elemental and spiritual forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the deeply venerated dead who are purposefully anchored to the land to act as eternal guardians of the lineage.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are sovereign, high creator gods who established the world but are generally withdrawn, requiring specific ritual intermediaries.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions venerate localized, territorial nature spirits that directly control the fertility and safety of the land, requiring active appeasement by local inhabitants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A geometric cosmogram charting the flow of spiritual energy, human incarnation, and the soul's initiatory journey.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct historical descent. Both traditions construct physical bundles of roots, minerals, and bones that serve as localized vessels for bound spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Hoodoo's use of grave dirt to command the dead derives directly from the Kongo use of mpemba (white clay or grave dirt) to connect with ancestors in minkisi creation.

GOLD SEAM: the withdrawn-creator shared with the BaKongo Nzambi a Mpungu — supreme, unmade, addressed almost never, the world handed to lesser spirits. The West/Central African deus otiosus across distinct branches.

GOLD SEAM: ancestors who recycle back into the lineage as newborns — Igbo ịlọ ụwa and Kongo bakulu both make death a revolving door within the bloodline rather than a terminus. Reincarnation-within-the-clan across distinct branches.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and theological descent from the Kongo supreme creator deity, brought to Jamaica via enslaved Central Africans.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Jamaican Obeah incorporates the Kongo technology of minkisi—binding spirits into physical botanical or mineral bundles to direct their power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The circumambulation around the Revivalist seal mirrors the Kongo Dikenga cosmogram, tracing the sun's journey and the barrier between the living and dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The powerful dead who remain active agents in their descendants' affairs, anchoring the community's spiritual architecture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme high god who establishes the world but recedes, leaving daily operations to spirits and ancestors.
Where Nzambi withdraws, the bisimbi fill the daily distance — the structural device of a remote high-god dispersed downward into the waters and stones within reach.
Kalunga IS the horizontal line of the dikenga — the water-axis dividing living from dead is one element of the same Kongo glyph.
The bakulu dwell across Kalunga in the inverted world below the water; the death-line and the ancestor-land are two faces of one Kongo cosmology.
The bisimbi inhabit the watery edge of Kalunga — the spirits of the threshold-water between the worlds.
An nkisi is animated by a captured spirit of the dead and packed with grave-earth; the bakulu are the very power the nkisi harnesses.
The nkisi folds the land of the dead into a single object — the cosmogram's two worlds made portable in the hand.
The clockwise turning of the cosmogram IS the cycle of the bakulu — souls descending below the water and returning above as newborns.
Two African high-gods who CREATE then RECEDE, leaving no daily cult — the deus-otiosus pattern shared across unrelated Niger-Congo branches (Akan and Bantu), structural kin not cognates.
Both supreme sky-powers held in awe at a remove while nearer spirits and ancestors receive the worship — high heaven distant by its own holiness.
An/Anu likewise drifts into remote authority while younger powers run the world — a parallel withdrawn-creator across families.
Both unmade, supreme, beyond image — but interpretive only: Nzambi recedes from cult where YHWH demands covenant. Flagged as a reach.
A cross / quartering at the centre of the world dividing the cosmos into four — the BaKongo sun's four moments against Eden's four rivers from one source. Quartered-cosmos parallel, no contact.

The fourfold quartered cosmos — four directional powers / four moments turning around a still centre — appears in both Kongo and Mesoamerica with no possible contact. A striking structural convergence.
Both are quartered-cosmos diagrams: a cross-in-a-circle whose four arms map the directions and the turning of time. The cosmogram and the gameboard as the same fourfold glyph.
Both set a vertical axis joining the worlds at the centre of a quartered space — but the dikenga's axis is a line of light and water, not a tree. A loose axis-mundi echo, flagged.
The water that must be crossed to reach the dead — Kalunga's mirror-line against the Styx that Charon ferries. Death as a watery passage across families.
Both govern an underworld reached by crossing waters, but Kalunga is a threshold-line and inversion rather than an enthroned lord of the dead — a loose echo, flagged.
The many lesser water-spirits who manage the world a withdrawn creator left behind — bisimbi and abosom are the same answer to a remote high-god, in neighbouring but distinct Niger-Congo branches.
A reach: both diffuse one sacred power into the many faces of the landscape — the one-through-many. Speculative, no contact.
Two African ancestor-cults where the honoured dead are the nearest sacred power, closer than the withdrawn creator — Kongo bakulu and Akan nsamanfo, functional kin across branches.
Both centre the dead as a living power and tie them to renewal, but the bakulu are promoted ancestors who recycle souls, not a single dying-rising god — a loose structural echo.
Both make a fashioned object/body into a true vessel of sacred presence — the nkisi inhabited by a captured dead, the ixiptla by a god. A reach across unrelated rites, flagged.

Male-and-female completeness as the ground of being — but where Yin-Yang holds the two in eternal balance, Mahungu is a wholeness SPLIT, complementarity remembered as a fall. The divergence sharpens the shared joint.
Both name a primal dual-gendered unity prior to division, but the parallel is interpretive and Mahungu is a first human rather than a creator-pair. Flagged as a reach.
The original androgyne / dual principle whose splitting begins the lived world — a loose echo across unrelated families, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A physical, handheld object that serves as a direct, empowered locus of spiritual force, circumventing distant, unapproachable gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme high god of Central Bantu cosmology who established the world's machinery but has since withdrawn completely to the sky.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Deeply structural parallel reflecting Bantu roots: localized territorial spirits inhabiting specific natural features that enforce ecological and moral taboos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Nature spirits intimately bound to specific localized geography (stones, streams) that are entirely distinct from human ancestors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are ritually empowered physical vessels housing active, sometimes dangerous spirits that require constant feeding.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Specific physical materials—minerals, herbs, animal parts—that are ritually awakened as active vessels for divine or spiritual resonance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The physical and ritual veneration of formidable, heavy ancestral presences that anchor and protect the community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and cultural survival of the supreme Kongo creator god in the Cuban diaspora.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Palo nganga is a direct, expanded evolution of the Kongo nkisi—a vessel that physically houses and utilizes spiritual power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Draws on Kongo ancestral logic, though Palo focuses specifically on the magical pact with physical bones rather than general bloodline ancestors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Kongo cosmogram physically survived and evolved into the intricate patipembas (drawn magical seals) of Palo.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Territorial nature spirits residing in the landscape who manage the earth's fertility and demand localized appeasement.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A direct continuation of Kongo vessel magic, where spirits are physically housed in empowered containers of earth and blood.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmology of Quimbanda's realms heavily adapts the Kongo cosmogram's division of the physical and spiritual worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Venerated, highly formalized representations of ancestors who anchor the living community and guarantee its fertility.
Two southern-African remote creators worshipped less by petition than presupposed behind the lived ritual; the supreme maker as a horizon rather than a recipient of prayer.

Both encode the crossing between the world of the living and the world of the dead as the heart of practice — the Kongo cosmogram's mirrored worlds and the San healer's nightly passage out and back along his own spine.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The ultimate creator deity who established the cosmos and then withdrew, leaving the active governance of nature to localized spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Innumerable localized territorial spirits that inhabit specific rocks, pools, and forests, demanding constant propitiation from those who use the land.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Venerated family ancestors who act as the ultimate moral arbiters and unseen co-residents within the earthly community.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel where a specialized ritual is meticulously constructed as a 'shield' to protect the physical body from hostile external forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme high god who establishes the universe but remains deeply withdrawn, leaving daily operations to spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The intimately involved lineage dead who continuously govern the moral and physical welfare of their descendants.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Non-lineage, localized spirits that temporarily attach to human hosts to bestow specialized esoteric knowledge or skills.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A physically forged, ritually charged artifact that acts as a technology to actively house and command unseen spiritual forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A structural parallel where practitioners work intimately with powerful, morally ambiguous spirits housed in specific vessels or sigils to enact material change.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A Bantu-rooted supreme being, utterly transcendent and removed from daily petition.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancestral spirits who mediate between the living community and the high god.
GOLD SEAM: The Congolese cosmogram directly informs the spatial and spiritual geometry of the Haitian peristyle and the intersecting planes of existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The high god whose singular authority governs the cosmos without the need for physical idols or pantheonic competitors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The transcendent, ultimately unknowable supreme being of Afro-diasporic cosmologies who acts through lesser spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Reincarnated ancestral presences who actively return to guide, protect, and enforce lineage law among the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The intense veneration of a physical, localized container that houses the active spiritual presence and authority of the ancestors.

dossier.essence: The religion of the BaKongo, a Bantu people of west-central Africa (the Democratic Republic of Congo, Republic of Congo, Angola, and Cabinda), heirs of the Kingdom of Kongo that arose by the 14th century. Living and hugely influential across the Atlantic diaspora, it centers on a withdrawn creator, Nzambi a Mpungu; the ancestors (bakulu); local water- and nature-spirits; and ritual power-objects (minkisi) directed by the nganga expert.
dossier.cosmos: Reality is diagrammed by the dikenga (the Kongo cosmogram, or yowa cross): a circle quartered by the sun's four moments, with the land of the living above and the inverted land of the dead below, divided by Kalunga — the water-line that mirrors and reverses. Death is not a fall but a crossing and inversion; the dead cross Kalunga, dwell white-skinned in the world below, and return as newborns, so the cosmos is one revolving wheel of souls. Nzambi a Mpungu, too vast to be approached, leaves no cult, while the spring- and river-spirits (bisimbi) fill the nearer distance.
dossier.practice: The nganga constructs and directs minkisi — containers packed with medicines and grave-earth and animated by a captured spirit of the dead (the nail-driven nkondi its famous form) — to heal, judge, or strike; ancestors are consulted and fed, and local bisimbi propitiated. Historic Kongo also absorbed Catholicism from 1491, producing enduring syncretisms carried into Cuba, Haiti, Brazil, and the American South.
dossier.canon: Oral tradition, preserved above all in the early-20th-century Kikongo texts written by Kongo catechists for Karl Laman, and in Wyatt MacGaffey's translations of nganga testimony.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds death as inversion and return — the dead not a terminus but the supply of souls turning back into the living, and the sacred engineered into a graspable object rather than petitioned from a far heaven.
sources: Wyatt MacGaffey, 'Religion and Society in Central Africa: The BaKongo of Lower Zaire' (1986)
Robert Farris Thompson, 'Flash of the Spirit: African and Afro-American Art and Philosophy' (1983)
John M. Janzen & Wyatt MacGaffey, 'An Anthology of Kongo Religion' (1974)
